project_title_en: Repayment Assistance Plan – Enhanced Verification Model (RAP-EVM)
description:
In November 2023, the Canada Student Financial Assistance (CSFA) Program
introduced a change to the application verification system that supports the
Repayment Assistance Plan (RAP) called the Repayment Assistance Plan -
Enhanced Verification Model (RAP-EVM).
RAP-EVM is designed to verify the information declared by borrowers, which
are all factors in determining RAP eligibility and calculating RAP payment
requirements, during the RAP application process (i.e. income, family size and
marital status) against taxpayer information provided by the Canada Revenue
Agency (CRA). RAP-EVM applies pre-established business rules to identify
applications that meet the selection criteria. Selection criteria were
determined using a risk-based sampling methodology to identify high-risk
groups more likely to misreport information. The creation of risk group
categories was undertaken through statistical analysis of previous cases, in
collaboration with ESDC’s Integrity Services Branch. Based on this analysis,
CSFA Program identified borrowers with inconsistent family size changes
(e.g., if a borrower were to change their response between RAP applications
to from a family size of one to a family size above one, and then revert to
family size of one), borrowers who claim zero income, and borrowers who
report their income just under the RAP zero payment threshold.
Once selected for verification, a request for taxpayer information (i.e. income,
marital status, number of dependants) on these applicants is made to the
CRA. If the application data matches CRA taxpayer data, the application is
adjudicated for an approval or refusal for RAP. If there are discrepancies, an
agent with ESDC’s third-party Service Provider (SP), which operates under
banner of the National Student Loans Service Centre (NSLSC), will undertake
further verification directly with the borrower. The borrower will then be
required to submit additional proof substantiating their information, which an
agent will review bef
qa_section_text:
Section 3.1: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making process?
(Check all that apply)
Improve overall quality of decisions
Use innovative approaches
2. What client needs will the system address and how will this system meet them? If possible,
describe how client needs have been identified.
RAP-EVM was implemented to increase the integrity of the CSFA Program
RAP benefit following the Office of the Auditor General’s 2020 audit report,
which recommended that ESDC take appropriate action to ensure a systemic
verification of RAP applications to maximize student loans repayments.
However, with the implementation of this new system, clients that are
selected for verification could potentially experience faster processing times
due to RAP-EVM optimizing the application verification process.
3. Please describe any public benefits the system is expected to have.
In 2022-2023, approximately 283,000 borrowers benefited from RAP, and of
those, 90% of applicants were approved for a $0 affordable payment. Given
the extent of borrowers that benefit from RAP, the introduction of RAP-EVM is
anticipated to help ensure program integrity and sound stewardship of public
funds. ESDC will make sure that only eligible applicants receive RAP benefits,
reducing the potential for misrepresentation and misuse of taxpayer funds.
4.How effective will the system likely be in meeting client needs?
Very effective [ Points: +0 ]
5. Please describe any improvements, benefits, or advantages you expect from using an
automated system. This could include relevant program indicators and performance targets.
RAP-EVM could result in the following outcomes:
- The new partially automated RAP-EVM system allows the program and
service provider to easily identify RAP trends, which ultimately helps to inform
evidence-based policy changes.
-Reduced instances of ineligible applicants receiving RAP benefits
-Reduced frequency of misrepresentation
6. Please describe how you will ensure that the system is confined to addressing the client
needs identified above.
Each of the CSFA Program’s systems is governed by guidelines with respect to
whom is allowed to access personal information. Specifically, each system
has clearly defined user roles to control access to the database, including the
use of unique user identifiers and permission settings to view, modify and
delete information contained in the database.
Access to taxpayer information provided under the MOU will be controlled and
limited to ESDC and SP personnel who:
• Occupy a position that requires access to this information;
• Have a valid Reliability Security Screening Clearance; and
• Have been briefed on the provisions of section 241 and section 239 of the
Income Tax Act to maintain the confidentiality of taxpayer information, as well
as security measures to safeguard this information as required.
Authorized personnel with Reliability Security Screening Clearance include the
following positions: RAP back-office agents, managers and data analysts at
the SP, CSFA Program data analysts, and CSFA Program staff working on RAP.
Borrowers’ personal data are not allowed to be stored in the local hard drives
of PCs, laptops and portable devices.
7. Please describe any trade-offs between client interests and program objectives that you have
considered during the design of the project.
RAP-EVM may increase timeliness by eliminating the need for a client to
provide additional documentation when selected for verification, if no
discrepancies between reported information and CRA information are found.
8. Have alternative non-automated processes been considered?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
9. If non-automated processes were considered, why was automation identified as the preferred
option?
RAP-EVM replaces the previous, non-authoritative source verification system,
which required selected applicants to provide documents to prove that their
reported income was accurate; RAP-EVM only requires a borrower to submit
additional proof when a discrepancy is identified following the exchange with
CRA. Automation using RAP-EVM was the preferred option to decrease the
administrative burden on applicants who have no discrepancies between their
reported income and proof of income provided by CRA.
10. What would be the consequence of not deploying the system?
Service cannot be delivered in a timely or efficient manner [ Points: +2 ]
Other (please specify) [ Points: 0 ]
11. Please describe
The Office of the Auditor General (OAG) 2020 Spring Report called on ESDC to
“ensure proper, systematic verification of RAP applications in order to
maximize the repayment of student loan.” Failure to deploy the RAP-EVM
system would limit the program’s long-term sustainability.
Risk Profile
12. Is the project within an area of intense public scrutiny (e.g. because of privacy concerns)
and/or frequent litigation?
No [ Points: +0 ]

_full_text:
Algorithmic Impact Assessment Results
Version: 0.10.0
Project Details
1. Name of Respondent
Megan Ellis
2. Job Title
Manager
3. Department
Employment and Social Development (Department of)
4. Branch
Learning
5. Project Title
Repayment Assistance Plan – Enhanced Verification Model (RAP-EVM)
6. Project ID from IT Plan
N/A
7. Departmental Program (from Department Results Framework)
Canada Student Financial Assistance Program and Canada Apprentice Loan
8. Project Phase
Implementation [ Points: 0 ]
9. Please provide a project description:
In November 2023, the Canada Student Financial Assistance (CSFA) Program
introduced a change to the application verification system that supports the
Repayment Assistance Plan (RAP) called the Repayment Assistance Plan -
Enhanced Verification Model (RAP-EVM).
RAP-EVM is designed to verify the information declared by borrowers, which
are all factors in determining RAP eligibility and calculating RAP payment
requirements, during the RAP application process (i.e. income, family size and
marital status) against taxpayer information provided by the Canada Revenue
Agency (CRA). RAP-EVM applies pre-established business rules to identify
applications that meet the selection criteria. Selection criteria were
determined using a risk-based sampling methodology to identify high-risk
groups more likely to misreport information. The creation of risk group
categories was undertaken through statistical analysis of previous cases, in
collaboration with ESDC’s Integrity Services Branch. Based on this analysis,
CSFA Program identified borrowers with inconsistent family size changes
(e.g., if a borrower were to change their response between RAP applications
to from a family size of one to a family size above one, and then revert to
family size of one), borrowers who claim zero income, and borrowers who
report their income just under the RAP zero payment threshold.
Once selected for verification, a request for taxpayer information (i.e. income,
marital status, number of dependants) on these applicants is made to the
CRA. If the application data matches CRA taxpayer data, the application is
adjudicated for an approval or refusal for RAP. If there are discrepancies, an
agent with ESDC’s third-party Service Provider (SP), which operates under
banner of the National Student Loans Service Centre (NSLSC), will undertake
further verification directly with the borrower. The borrower will then be
required to submit additional proof substantiating their information, which an
agent will review before making a decision for approval or refusal of the
application.
RAP-EVM replaces the previous, non-authoritative source verification system,
which required selected applicants to provide documents to prove that their
reported income was accurate; RAP-EVM only requires a borrower to submit
additional proof when a discrepancy is identified following the exchange with
CRA.
The exchange of borrower and taxpayer information between the SP and the
CRA is governed by a Memorandum of Understanding (MOU) which outlines
the agreement on the information to be exchanged between ESDC and the
CRA as well as the conditions and procedures that will apply to the exchange
of information. RAP-EVM’s data exchange process between the SP and the
CRA is handled via a secure file transfer, known as the Managed Secure File
Transfer. Following the exchange of data between CRA and the SP, the RAP
application adjudication takes place in a closed environment. Personal
information collected and used for the purposes of RAP-EVM is safeguarded
under ESDC’s and the CRA’s legal authorities and related policies and
directives for collecting and using personal information.
There are two circumstances under which a borrower may wish to have their
RAP decision re-evaluated. Reconsideration is for borrowers that have been
refused but can show that unforeseen and unavoidable circumstances have
caused them or their spouse or common-law partner to incur exceptional
expenses that prevent them from fulfilling their repayment obligations. The
other recourse is re-adjudication, which occurs when a borrower has been
approved for RAP with a monthly Affordable Payment (AP) but indicates that
their gross monthly income has significantly dropped and that they are no
longer able to make their AP.
The Program has completed a Privacy Impact Assessment which was sent to
the Office of the Privacy Commissioner of Canada (OPC) in November 2023.
Upon review, the OPC recommended that the CFSA Program conduct an
Algorithmic Impact Assessment, as the creation of risk group categories in an
automated verification process falls within the scope of the Directive on
Automated Decision-Making.
About The System
10. Please check which of the following capabilities apply to your system.
Process optimization and workflow automation: Analyzing large data sets to
identify and anomalies, cluster patterns, predict outcomes or ways to
optimize; and automate specific workflows

Section 2: Requirements Specific to Impact Level 1

None

None
Notice
None
Human-in-the-loop for decisions
Decisions may be rendered without direct human involvement.
Explanation
In addition to any applicable legal requirement, ensure that a meaningful explanation is published
for common decision results. The explanation must provide a general description of:
the role of the system in the decision-making process;
input data, its source and method of collection;
the criteria used to evaluate input data and the operations applied to process it;
the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed to interpret it in the
context of the administrative decision; and
the principal factors behind a decision.
Explanations must also inform clients of relevant recourse options, where appropriate.
Descriptions must be made available in plain language through the Algorithmic Impact
Assessment and discoverable via a departmental website.
Training
None
IT and business continuity management
None
Approval for the system to operate
None
Other requirements
The Directive on Automated Decision-Making also includes other requirements that must be met
for all impact levels.
Directive on Automated Decision-Making
Contact your institution's ATIP office to discuss the requirement for a Privacy Impact
Assessment as per the Directive on Privacy Impact Assessment.
Section 3: Questions and Answers
Section 3.1: Impact Questions and Answers
Reasons for Automation
1. What is motivating your team to introduce automation into this decision-making process?
(Check all that apply)
Improve overall quality of decisions
Use innovative approaches
2. What client needs will the system address and how will this system meet them? If possible,
describe how client needs have been identified.
RAP-EVM was implemented to increase the integrity of the CSFA Program
RAP benefit following the Office of the Auditor General’s 2020 audit report,
which recommended that ESDC take appropriate action to ensure a systemic
verification of RAP applications to maximize student loans repayments.
However, with the implementation of this new system, clients that are
selected for verification could potentially experience faster processing times
due to RAP-EVM optimizing the application verification process.
3. Please describe any public benefits the system is expected to have.
In 2022-2023, approximately 283,000 borrowers benefited from RAP, and of
those, 90% of applicants were approved for a $0 affordable payment. Given
the extent of borrowers that benefit from RAP, the introduction of RAP-EVM is
anticipated to help ensure program integrity and sound stewardship of public
funds. ESDC will make sure that only eligible applicants receive RAP benefits,
reducing the potential for misrepresentation and misuse of taxpayer funds.
4.How effective will the system likely be in meeting client needs?
Very effective [ Points: +0 ]
5. Please describe any improvements, benefits, or advantages you expect from using an
automated system. This could include relevant program indicators and performance targets.
RAP-EVM could result in the following outcomes:
- The new partially automated RAP-EVM system allows the program and
service provider to easily identify RAP trends, which ultimately helps to inform
evidence-based policy changes.
-Reduced instances of ineligible applicants receiving RAP benefits
-Reduced frequency of misrepresentation
6. Please describe how you will ensure that the system is confined to addressing the client
needs identified above.
Each of the CSFA Program’s systems is governed by guidelines with respect to
whom is allowed to access personal information. Specifically, each system
has clearly defined user roles to control access to the database, including the
use of unique user identifiers and permission settings to view, modify and
delete information contained in the database.
Access to taxpayer information provided under the MOU will be controlled and
limited to ESDC and SP personnel who:
• Occupy a position that requires access to this information;
• Have a valid Reliability Security Screening Clearance; and
• Have been briefed on the provisions of section 241 and section 239 of the
Income Tax Act to maintain the confidentiality of taxpayer information, as well
as security measures to safeguard this information as required.
Authorized personnel with Reliability Security Screening Clearance include the
following positions: RAP back-office agents, managers and data analysts at
the SP, CSFA Program data analysts, and CSFA Program staff working on RAP.
Borrowers’ personal data are not allowed to be stored in the local hard drives
of PCs, laptops and portable devices.
7. Please describe any trade-offs between client interests and program objectives that you have
considered during the design of the project.
RAP-EVM may increase timeliness by eliminating the need for a client to
provide additional documentation when selected for verification, if no
discrepancies between reported information and CRA information are found.
8. Have alternative non-automated processes been considered?
Yes [ Points: +0 ]
9. If non-automated processes were considered, why was automation identified as the preferred
option?
RAP-EVM replaces the previous, non-authoritative source verification system,
which required selected applicants to provide documents to prove that their
reported income was accurate; RAP-EVM only requires a borrower to submit
additional proof when a discrepancy is identified following the exchange with
CRA. Automation using RAP-EVM was the preferred option to decrease the
administrative burden on applicants who have no discrepancies between their
reported income and proof of income provided by CRA.
10. What would be the consequence of not deploying the system?
Service cannot be delivered in a timely or efficient manner [ Points: +2 ]
Other (please specify) [ Points: 0 ]
11. Please describe
The Office of the Auditor General (OAG) 2020 Spring Report called on ESDC to
“ensure proper, systematic verification of RAP applications in order to
maximize the repayment of student loan.” Failure to deploy the RAP-EVM
system would limit the program’s long-term sustainability.
Risk Profile
12. Is the project within an area of intense public scrutiny (e.g. because of privacy concerns)
and/or frequent litigation?
No [ Points: +0 ]
13. Are clients in this line of business particularly vulnerable?
No [ Points: +0 ]
14. Are stakes of the decisions very high?
No [ Points: +0 ]
15. Will this project have major impacts on staff, either in terms of their numbers or their roles?
No [ Points: +0 ]
16. Will the use of the system create or exacerbate barriers for persons with disabilities?
No [ Points: +0 ]
Project Authority
17. Will you require new policy authority for this project?

About the Algorithm
18. The algorithm used will be a (trade) secret
No [ Points: +0 ]
19. The algorithmic process will be difficult to interpret or to explain
No [ Points: +0 ]
About the Decision
20. Please describe the decision(s) that will be automated.
The following outlines the process flow of RAP-EVM:
1. Application: The borrower submits their RAP application to the SP,
indicating their previous month or current month’s income, married/common-
law status and number of dependants.
a. On the RAP application, the borrower is informed through their consent
that their data can be exchanged with the CRA.
b. If the borrower has indicated that they are in a married or common-law
relationship, then consent, name, date of birth, SIN, and income of their
partner will be provided to the SP. A signed consent of the borrower and their
spouse must be received prior to any data exchange between the SP and CRA.
Spousal signature is a prerequisite for the adjudication of the RAP application.
c. If the application is complete, SP calculates whether a borrower is eligible
for RAP.
2. Selection: Those borrowers that have been identified as high risk for
misrepresentation based on the risk-based sampling methods may be
selected for verification using the RAP-EVM. Borrowers that are not selected
for verification are assessed through the regular RAP process, including
calculation (#9). RAP calculation precedes selection.
3. Request for Information: The SP securely sends an electronic request for
information to the CRA, including the name, SIN, and date of birth of the
borrower and the borrower’s married/common-law partner (if applicable), to
confirm previous year’s income (Line 15000 of the borrower’s tax return),
marital status, and the number of dependants in the family.
4. Response to Request: The CRA provides the aforementioned borrower's
tax data to the SP.
a. The response would occur via a secure electronic file transmission.
b. The response would include the names, date of birth, and SIN of
borrowers (and their married/common-law partner, if applicable) as well as
their Line 15000 income, number of dependants and marital status. The CRA
will also confirm the tax year of the data (the most recent data from the last
two years requested only). If the response returns with no data, adjudication
process moves to #7.
5. Data Comparison: The SP compares Line 15000 of the borrower’s income
data , as provided by the CRA to the borrower’s reported income on their RAP
application.
a. Disparities beyond a “threshold of tolerance” in income between a
borrower’s CRA tax data and the income declared on the RAP application
would justify a request for additional proof from the borrower. The same
would apply for inconsistencies with declarations of ‘married’ or ‘single’ and
instances where borrowers report a higher number of dependants on their
RAP application. The “threshold of tolerance” is determined by the CSFA
Program.
b. The SP annualizes RAP application income data before comparison with
CRA borrower (and spouse/common-law partner) income data.
c. If a family size discrepancy is identified, but the verified family income is
less than the family income threshold, additional proof would not be
requested since it would not make a difference for adjudication.
6. Assessment Part One: If the borrower’s declared CRA income, family size
and married/common-law status are consistent with the information reported
on the RAP application, or if business rules deem no further validation
required, the borrower will be assessed for RAP under the existing process.
7. Assessment Part Two: If there is a variance between their RAP application
data and their CRA tax data, or if the CRA is unable to provide the borrower's
tax data, the borrower is asked to provide additional substantiating
documentation.
8. Assessment Part Three: Based on the borrower’s declared income and
any supporting documentation, the SP proceeds to finalize the assessment of
the borrower’s RAP application.
9. Final Calculation and Decision: The SP runs a final calculation of eligibility,
based on verified family income and size, unchanged or revised based on the
requested borrower documentation. If the borrower provides additional proof
documentation that differs from their RAP application data, their RAP benefit
is re-calculated based on the new information.
10. End of Process: The borrower is informed of the status of their
application.
21. Does the decision pertain to any of the categories below (check all that apply):
Economic interests (grants and contributions, tax benefits, debt collection) [ Points: +1 ]
Impact Assessment
22. Which of the following best describes the type of automation you are planning?
Partial automation (the system will contribute to administrative decision-
making by supporting an officer through assessments, recommendations,
intermediate decisions, or other outputs) [ Points: +2 ]
23. Please describe the role of the system in the decision-making process.
When an electronic or paper application is received at the SP, the system
adjudicates the information using business rules to determine eligibility for
RAP. A second set of business rules is triggered when a RAP application is
approved to determine whether income, number of dependants, and marital
status verification is required using the RAP-EVM. Once data submitted by the
borrower has been compared to CRA data, the system then determines if
proof from the borrower is required to confirm the information submitted.
Throughout these processes, the system uses business rules in the decision-
making processes.
24. Will the system be making decisions or assessments that require judgement or discretion?
No [ Points: +0 ]
25. Please describe the criteria used to evaluate client data and the operations applied to
process it.
There is a RAP formula in the Canada Student Financial Assistance
Regulations (CSFAR), which includes family income, family size, and loan
balance. The CSFA Program has developed effective methods to identify high-
risk groups that are more prone to misreporting information. The program
engages RAP-EVM to verify the data of these high-risk groups, as well as of
individuals selected for data verification through random sampling.
26. Please describe the output produced by the system and any relevant information needed to
interpret it in the context of the administrative decision.
For RAP-EVM, if the borrower income differs from that at CRA, greater than
the allowable variance, a letter is generated to the borrower requesting their
proof of income. The same applies to marital status and number of
dependants. If the information at CRA differs, proof must be provided by the
borrower. In these automated processes, there is no interpretation required.
27. Will the system perform an assessment or other operation that would not otherwise be
completed by a human?
No [ Points: +0 ]
28. Is the system used by a different part of the organization than the ones who developed it?
No [ Points: +0 ]
29. Are the impacts resulting from the decision reversible?
Reversible [ Points: +1 ]
30. How long will impacts from the decision last?
Impacts are most likely to be brief [ Points: +1 ]
31. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
Impacts from the decision are time limited since RAP approval periods last for
six months at a time. During this period, an applicant can always request a re-
adjudication due to changes in their income situation, in which case an agent
can make changes to the initial RAP calculation decision, provided all the
necessary documentation is received. In the event that an applicant receives a
RAP refusal, the impact of a refusal is also most likely to be time limited, as
clients whose applications are refused may re-apply when/if they meet the
requirements as outlined by the CSFAR. An applicant may also appeal a
decision on RAP eligibility by submitting exceptional expenses.
32. The impacts that the decision will have on the rights or freedoms of individuals will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
33. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system only seeks to validate borrower provided information on a RAP
application against an authoritative source (i.e., CRA tax information).
Given that only borrowers who satisfy the eligibility criteria, as outlined by the
CSFAR, should be receiving RAP benefits, the only impact of the system is that
it strengthens the integrity of the CSFA Program.
34. The impacts that the decision will have on the equality, dignity, privacy, and autonomy of
individuals will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
35. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system only seeks to validate borrower provided information on a RAP
application against an authoritative source (i.e., CRA tax information).
Given that only borrowers who satisfy the eligibility criteria, as outlined by the
CSFAR, should be receiving RAP benefits, the only impact of the system is that
it strengthens the integrity of the CSFA Program.
36. The impacts that the decision will have on the health and well-being of individuals will likely
be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
37. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system only seeks to validate borrower provided information on a RAP
application against an authoritative source (i.e., CRA tax information).
Given that only borrowers who satisfy the eligibility criteria, as outlined by the
CSFAR, should be receiving RAP benefits, the only impact of the system is that

38. The impacts that the decision will have on the economic interests of individuals will likely
be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
39. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system only seeks to validate borrower provided information on a RAP

Given that only borrowers who satisfy the eligibility criteria, as outlined by the

40. The impacts that the decision will have on the ongoing sustainability of an environmental
ecosystem, will likely be:
Little to no impact [ Points: +1 ]
41. Please describe why the impacts resulting from the decision are as per selected option
above.
The system only seeks to validate borrower provided information on a RAP
application against an authoritative source (i.e., CRA tax information). The
only impact of the system is that it strengthens the integrity of the CSFA
Program by ensuring that only borrowers who meet the eligibility criteria
described in the CSFAR receive RAP benefits.
About the Data - A. Data Source

54. Please describe the input data collected and used by the system, its source, and method of
collection.
The input data includes personal information, gross family income, marital
status, and number of dependants provided by RAP applicants during the
application process. Borrowers can apply for RAP either online or by
submitting paper forms. During the exchange, CRA returns a file containing
SIN, date of birth, last name, first name, taxation years, income, marital status,
and number of dependents.
About the Data - B. Type of Data
